content: HMM REGIME DETECTION OVERVIEW: Hidden Markov Models classify market into regimes (Crash/Bear/Neutral/Bull/Euphoria). HMMs don't predict prices — they predict the TYPE of ENVIRONMENT. The system detects regimes and adjusts exposure/strategies BEFORE applying trading logic. The HMM is the BRAIN of the system — it tells every other component what kind of market we're in.

HMM FEATURE ENGINEERING: Input features include price action metrics, volume metrics, and volatility metrics. These are computed as technical indicators and fed to the HMM as observation sequences. The quality of regime detection depends entirely on feature selection — garbage features = garbage regimes. Features should capture the ESSENTIAL market state: trend strength, volatility level, volume participation.

HMM TRAINING PARAMETERS: Train on approximately 2 YEARS of daily data. Test 3-7 regimes (3, 4, 5, 6, 7) and pick the OPTIMAL count using statistical criteria (BIC — Bayesian Information Criterion, or AIC — Akaike Information Criterion). Don't hardcode the regime count — let the data tell you. The model with the lowest BIC is the best fit. More regimes = more flexibility but risk of overfitting.

REGIME LABELING: Regimes are sorted by MEAN RETURN and labeled accordingly. With 3 regimes: Bear, Neutral, Bull. With 4: Crash, Bear, Bull, Euphoria. With 5: Crash, Bear, Neutral, Bull, Euphoria. The sorting ensures consistent labeling across training runs — regime 0 is always the worst performer, regime N is always the best. Without sorting, labels would be random across runs.

CRITICAL — AVOIDING LOOK-AHEAD BIAS: The default predict() function in the HMM library processes the ENTIRE sequence of data, which creates look-ahead bias — it uses future data to classify the current state. This makes backtests look amazing but is UNTRADABLE in live markets. Instead, use the FORWARD ALGORITHM ONLY — processing data point by point without seeing future data. This is a MANDATORY requirement.

REGIME STABILITY FILTER: A regime must persist for at least 3 CONSECUTIVE BARS before the system acts on it. If the regime flickers between states more than 4 times in the past 20 bars, it's logged as UNCERTAINTY and position sizes are REDUCED. This prevents whipsawing — reacting to every minor regime change. The filter acts as a low-pass filter on regime signals.

WALK-FORWARD BACKTESTING — HOW IT WORKS: In-sample window = 252 trading days (1 year). Out-of-sample window = ~6 months for evaluation. The window SLIDES FORWARD: retrain on new in-sample, test on new out-of-sample, iterate. Traditional backtesting uses ALL data to find perfect settings = overfitted, results cannot be replicated. Walk-forward tests on UNSEEN data = realistic out-of-sample performance.

BACKTESTING REALISTIC SIMULATION: The backtester includes slippage modeling (realistic execution costs) and transaction costs (commissions and spread). Results are broken down by REGIME (how strategy performs in each market condition) and by CONFIDENCE BUCKET (whether high-confidence trades outperform low-confidence). Without slippage and fees, backtests are meaningless.

BENCHMARK COMPARISONS: Compare strategy against: 1) BUY AND HOLD — simply holding the asset. 2) 200-DAY SMA TREND FOLLOWING — one of the most common systemic strategies. 3) RANDOM ENTRY — random entries and allocation changes with same risk rules. If your strategy can't beat random entry with the same risk rules, it has no edge. Stress test with random crash events (10-15% single-day drops).

CIRCUIT BREAKERS — THE MOST IMPORTANT SYSTEM: More important than the HMMs. A mediocre strategy with great risk management only loses money a little bit. A great strategy with bad risk management can BLOW UP an account. Circuit breakers work INDEPENDENTLY of the AI model — even if strategies are technically correct, these hardcoded limits protect against catastrophic losses.

CIRCUIT BREAKER THRESHOLDS: Down 2% in single day → all position sizes CUT IN HALF. Down 3% in single day → CLOSE EVERYTHING. Down 5% in a week → half position sizes. Down 10% from PEAK → bot STOPS completely, writes a BLOCK FILE. The block file is DELIBERATE — you must manually investigate what happened, understand why, and CONSCIOUSLY delete the file to resume. This FORCES accountability.

POSITION-LEVEL RISK: Max risk per trade = 1% of portfolio. Leverage limits = configurable per strategy. Order validation = pre-trade checks before execution. Correlation checks = prevents entering correlated positions (e.g., long BTC and long ETH simultaneously — same risk factor). All of these work independently of the AI — the AI cannot override them.

VOLATILITY-BASED ALLOCATION: Low volatility → 95% allocation, 1.25x leverage, fully invested, maximum exposure. Medium volatility → variable allocation, 1.0x leverage, stay invested if trend is intact. High volatility → minimal allocation, no leverage, reduce exposure, capital preservation. The allocation layer is directly influenced by the regime brain — it changes HOW MUCH capital to deploy per regime.

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE (5 Components): 1) BRAIN — HMM regime detection classifies market. 2) ALLOCATION — volatility-based strategies determine capital per regime. 3) SAFETY — circuit breakers, drawdown limits, leverage controls (independent). 4) BROKERAGE — Alpaca API for real trade execution. 5) DASHBOARD — Streamlit UI for monitoring. Paper trade for AT LEAST ONE MONTH before going live.

MAIN LOOP ORCHESTRATION: 1) Load configuration. 2) Connect to brokerage, verify account. 3) Check market hours. 4) Train HMMs on historical data. 5) Initialize risk manager and position tracker. 6) Start data feeds. 7) Main loop runs on each bar close (default: 5-minute bars). Error handling: brokerage API downtime, HMM computation errors, data feed drops — all logged, engine continues.

DASHBOARD MONITORING: Portfolio section = portfolio value, buying power, open positions. Regime Detection = current regime, confidence score, regime history. Risk Status = circuit breaker states, leverage usage, drawdown levels. Signal Feed = historical trades with allocation, entry prices, stops, P&L. Charts = price action with regime overlays, volume, confidence over time. All trades logged with structured monitoring.

PERFORMANCE METRICS: Total Return (cumulative), Sharpe Ratio (risk-adjusted return), Max Drawdown (largest peak-to-trough), Win Rate (% profitable), Total Trades. Results broken down by regime (Crash/Bear/Neutral/Bull/Euphoria performance) and by confidence bucket. The regime breakdown shows which conditions your strategy handles well and which destroy it.

STRESS TESTING: Inject random crash events (10-15% single-day drops) into backtest to verify the system handles extreme conditions. If the system survives crashes with acceptable drawdown, it's robust. If crashes blow up the account, the position sizing or circuit breakers need tightening. Always stress test BEFORE going live.

CUSTOMIZATION POINTS: Tickers — change in settings file. Strategies — replace allocation logic in regime_strategies.rs. Risk Limits — adjust circuit breaker thresholds. Time Frames — change bar size (1m, 5m, 15m). The framework is GENERAL-PURPOSE — customize to your specific use case, risk tolerance, and asset class. Don't change the architecture, change the parameters.

ITERATIVE IMPROVEMENT: Review EVERY single rebalance decision. Run backtests across different tickers and time periods. Iterate on allocation parameters and strategies. Use Claude Code to continually improve the system. The bot automatically generates documentation for every file. Documentation contains complete system overview for reference. Tools like Claude Code opened possibilities for retail traders to build quantitative systems previously never accessible.
